title: Best Cat Grooming Brushes (2026) – Reduce Shedding & Hairballs
seoTitle: Best Cat Grooming Brushes (2026) – Tested on 6 Coat Types
seoDescription: We tested 9 cat brushes across short, medium, and long-haired breeds. Find the best deshedding tool, slicker brush, and grooming glove for your cat.
excerpt: Cat grooming brushes aren't one-size-fits-all. A brush that works perfectly on a short-haired tabby can pull painfully on a Persian's undercoat. We tested 9 popular cat brushes across six coat types — short single-coat, short double-coat, medium, long silky, long dense, and curly — to find which brushes genuinely reduce shedding and hairballs without stressing your cat. This guide covers brush types, technique, and how to make grooming a positive experience for even the most reluctant cats.
keywords: best brush for long haired cats

quickAnswer.reason: Most effective at removing loose undercoat hair in a single session. Reduces shedding by up to 90% when used weekly. Best for double-coated cats; use the slicker brush alternative for single-coated or sensitive cats.

buyingCriteria.description: Short-haired cats need soft bristle or rubber brushes. Long-haired cats need wide-tooth combs and slicker brushes. Double-coated breeds benefit from deshedding tools that reach the undercoat without cutting topcoat.

Wire pins work well for detangling but can scratch skin if pressed too hard. Rubber nubs massage while collecting loose hair. Stainless steel deshedding edges remove undercoat efficiently but must be used gently.
An uncomfortable brush means you won't use it consistently. Look for non-slip handles, appropriate size for your hand, and a weight that allows 10+ minutes of brushing without fatigue.

Some cats hate wire brushes but love grooming gloves. If your cat runs from the brush, try a different style rather than forcing the issue. Grooming gloves and soft rubber brushes have the highest acceptance rate.
Hair that clogs the brush and requires manual picking is frustrating. Self-cleaning slicker brushes with retractable pins and deshedding tools with push-button ejection save significant time.

sections.heading: Why Regular Brushing Matters for Cats
sections.content:
Cats groom themselves, but self-grooming has limits. Regular brushing provides three measurable benefits:

**Reduced hairballs.** When you remove loose fur with a brush, your cat swallows less during self-grooming. This directly reduces hairball frequency — a difference you'll notice within 2–3 weeks of consistent brushing.

**Less shedding around your home.** A 10-minute weekly brushing session removes the loose undercoat before it ends up on your furniture, clothes, and food. Double-coated breeds like Maine Coons and British Shorthairs shed heavily without regular intervention.

**Early health detection.** Brushing lets you spot skin issues, lumps, parasites, and wounds that hide under fur. Many cat health problems are caught during grooming sessions rather than vet visits.

Most cats can be trained to enjoy brushing if you start with short, positive sessions and use the right brush for their coat type.

sections.heading: Brush Types Explained
sections.content:
**Deshedding tools** (Furminator, DeShedding Edge) reach through the topcoat to remove loose undercoat hair. Extremely effective on double-coated breeds but can irritate single-coated cats or damage the topcoat if overused. Use once weekly maximum.

**Slicker brushes** have fine, angled wire pins set in a flat or curved pad. They detangle mats, remove loose hair, and work on most coat types. The Hertzko self-cleaning version has a button that retracts the pins for easy hair removal.

**Bristle brushes** are the gentlest option — soft natural or synthetic bristles distribute oils and smooth the coat. Best for short-haired, single-coated cats who don't need heavy deshedding.

**Pin brushes** have rounded-tip wire pins spaced widely apart. Good for long-haired cats to detangle without pulling. Less effective at removing loose undercoat.

**Grooming gloves** have rubber nubs on the palm and fingers. You pet your cat normally while the nubs collect loose hair. Lowest grooming efficiency but highest cat acceptance rate — ideal for cats who hate traditional brushes.

**Rubber curry brushes** (KONG ZoomGroom) use soft rubber teeth that massage while removing loose hair. Great for short-haired cats and bath time.

sections.heading: Best Brush by Cat Coat Type
sections.content:
**Short-haired, single coat** (Siamese, Burmese, Bengal):
Use a rubber curry brush or grooming glove 1–2x per week. These coats don't mat and shed minimally. A soft bristle brush adds shine.

**Short-haired, double coat** (British Shorthair, Russian Blue, American Shorthair):
Use a Furminator deshedding tool once weekly during shedding season, slicker brush for maintenance between sessions. These breeds shed more than their short coat suggests.

**Medium coat** (Domestic Medium Hair, Ragdoll developing coat):
Slicker brush 2–3x per week, with a wide-tooth comb to check for developing mats behind ears and under legs.

**Long, silky coat** (Turkish Angora, Balinese):
Pin brush daily for detangling, followed by a bristle brush for smoothing. These coats tangle quickly but don't mat as densely as Persian-type coats.

**Long, dense coat** (Persian, Himalayan, Maine Coon):
Wide-tooth comb first to separate and detangle. Then slicker brush to remove loose undercoat. Daily grooming is essential — skipping even 3–4 days leads to mats that require cutting.

**Curly/Rex coat** (Cornish Rex, Devon Rex, Selkirk Rex):
Gentle grooming glove only. Wire brushes damage the delicate curl structure. Brush minimally — once weekly at most.

sections.heading: How to Brush a Cat That Hates Being Brushed
sections.content:
Most brush-resistant cats had negative early experiences or are being brushed with the wrong tool. Here's how to reset:

**Step 1: Start with a grooming glove.** Most cats accept being petted with a glove even if they run from a brush. Use the glove for 2–3 weeks before introducing other tools.

**Step 2: Keep sessions short.** 2–3 minutes is enough initially. Stop before your cat shows signs of irritation (tail flicking, ear flattening, skin twitching).

**Step 3: Pair with treats.** Offer a high-value treat immediately after each brushing session. Some cats respond well to treats during brushing as well.

**Step 4: Brush in preferred areas first.** Most cats enjoy being brushed on their cheeks and chin. Start there, then gradually move to the back and sides. Save the belly and legs for last — or skip them entirely if your cat doesn't tolerate it.

**Step 5: Never force it.** Restraining a cat for brushing creates lasting negative associations. If your cat walks away, let them. Try again tomorrow.

For more enrichment strategies, see our guide on [how to entertain indoor cats](/guides/how-to-entertain-an-indoor-cat).

sections.heading: Dealing with Mats and Tangles
sections.content:
Mats are clumps of tangled, compressed fur that pull on your cat's skin and can cause irritation or infection underneath. Prevention is easier than removal.

**Prevention:**
- Brush long-haired cats daily, or at minimum every other day
- Focus on problem areas: behind ears, under front legs, belly, and the base of the tail
- Use a detangling spray for silky coats that tangle quickly

**Removing small mats:**
- Hold the mat at the base (near the skin) so pulling doesn't hurt
- Use a mat splitter or wide-tooth comb to work from the outer edge inward
- Never pull the mat away from the skin
- Work in 30-second intervals with breaks

**When to see a groomer:**
- Mats larger than a golf ball
- Mats directly against the skin (pelted)
- Multiple mats across the body
- Your cat becomes aggressive during mat removal

Professional groomers have specialized tools and techniques for safe mat removal. Severe matting may require sedated shaving at a veterinary clinic.

sections.heading: Grooming Schedule by Season
sections.content:
Cats shed more during spring and fall as their coat adjusts to temperature and daylight changes. Adjust your grooming routine seasonally:

**Spring (heavy shedding):**
- Double-coated cats: deshedding tool 2x/week + daily slicker brush
- Single-coated cats: grooming glove or rubber brush 3x/week
- Long-haired cats: daily combing + slicker brush

**Summer:**
- Maintain 2–3 brushing sessions per week
- Check for outdoor parasites if your cat has outdoor access
- Ensure cats in hot climates aren't developing heat-related skin issues under dense coats

**Fall (moderate shedding):**
- Similar to spring but slightly less intense
- Start increasing frequency in October as winter coat grows in

**Winter:**
- Reduce deshedding tool use to 1x/week — the undercoat provides necessary insulation
- Continue slicker brush or comb sessions to prevent mats
- Indoor heating can cause dry skin — look for excessive dandruff during brushing

commonMistakes.mistake: Using a deshedding tool on a single-coated cat
commonMistakes.fix: Deshedding tools are designed for double coats. On single-coated breeds, they can damage the topcoat and irritate skin. Use a rubber brush or grooming glove instead.

commonMistakes.mistake: Brushing too aggressively or pressing too hard
commonMistakes.fix: Let the brush do the work. Light, even strokes are more effective and comfortable. If you see skin reddening, you're pressing too hard.

commonMistakes.mistake: Only brushing the back and ignoring problem areas
commonMistakes.fix: Mats form behind ears, under legs, on the belly, and at the tail base. Gently brush these areas even if your cat is less cooperative.

commonMistakes.mistake: Waiting until mats form before brushing
commonMistakes.fix: Regular preventive brushing takes 5 minutes. Mat removal is stressful for both you and your cat. Prevention is always easier.

faq.question: How often should I brush my cat?
faq.answer: Short-haired cats: 1–2 times per week. Medium-haired cats: 2–3 times per week. Long-haired cats: daily. Increase frequency during spring and fall shedding seasons.

faq.question: What is the best brush for reducing cat shedding?
faq.answer: The Furminator deshedding tool is the most effective at removing loose undercoat. For cats who don't tolerate it, the Hertzko self-cleaning slicker brush is a good alternative. For brush-shy cats, start with a grooming glove.

faq.question: Can brushing help with cat hairballs?
faq.answer: Yes. Regular brushing removes loose fur before your cat ingests it during self-grooming. Most cat owners notice a significant reduction in hairball frequency within 2–3 weeks of consistent brushing.

faq.question: My cat hates being brushed. What should I do?
faq.answer: Start with a grooming glove instead of a traditional brush. Keep sessions under 3 minutes, brush only areas your cat enjoys (cheeks, chin, back), and reward with treats afterward. Gradually increase session length over several weeks.

faq.question: Is the Furminator safe for cats?
faq.answer: Yes, when used correctly. Use light pressure, brush in the direction of hair growth, and limit use to once per week. Do not use on cats with skin conditions, single coats, or irritated skin. Stop if you see redness.
